title: CDMGRestrictions

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_1 -- "CDMGRestrictions".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_1_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (statement)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition \ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}

\end{document}

% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. A manager

%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition \ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% No LN proof attached at graphs.tex:84-89 (the claim is a Remark);
% constructed from scratch from def-cdmg and not-cdmg.
Fix a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ and let $j \in J$.

\textbf{Sentence 1.} Let $v$ be any node in $G$. Suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By \refrow{def_3_2} this means either $j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $j \hut v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(v,j) \in E$. The directed-edges restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from \refrow{def_3_1} forces $j \in V$. But $j \in J$, contradicting the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ from \refrow{def_3_1}.
  \item If $j \huh v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(j,v) \in L$. The bidirected-edges restriction $L \ins V \times V$ from \refrow{def_3_1} forces $j \in V$ --- the same contradiction.
\end{itemize}
Hence $j \hus v \notin G$ for every node $v$, as claimed.

\textbf{Sentence 2.} Let $v$ be any node in $G$ and suppose $j \tuh v \in G$. By \refrow{def_3_2}, $(j,v) \in E$. The directed-edges restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from \refrow{def_3_1} then forces $v \in V$. This is the restrictive direction of the LN sentence ``edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed''; per the addition to the LN, this is permissive and no positive existence of such an edge is asserted.

\textbf{Sentence 3.} Let $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By \refrow{def_3_3} this means $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$, and by \refrow{def_3_2} one of three cases holds.
\begin{itemize}
  \item $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$: then $(j_1,j_2) \in E$ by \refrow{def_3_2}; the restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from \refrow{def_3_1} gives $j_2 \in V$, contradicting $j_2 \in J$ via the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ of \refrow{def_3_1}.
  \item $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$: then $(j_2,j_1) \in E$ by \refrow{def_3_2}; the same restriction from \refrow{def_3_1} gives $j_1 \in V$, contradicting $j_1 \in J$ via disjointness.
  \item $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$: then $(j_1,j_2) \in L$ by \refrow{def_3_2}; the restriction $L \ins V \times V$ from \refrow{def_3_1} gives $j_1 \in V$, again contradicting $j_1 \in J$.
\end{itemize}
Each case yields a contradiction, so no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — CDMGRestrictions

With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition \ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

Proof.

Fix a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ and let $j \in J$.

Sentence 1. Let $v$ be any node in $G$. Suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By def_3_2 this means either $j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.

  • If $j \hut v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(v,j) \in E$. The directed-edges restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from def_3_1 forces $j \in V$. But $j \in J$, contradicting the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ from def_3_1.
  • If $j \huh v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(j,v) \in L$. The bidirected-edges restriction $L \ins V \times V$ from def_3_1 forces $j \in V$ — the same contradiction.

Hence $j \hus v \notin G$ for every node $v$, as claimed.

Sentence 2. Let $v$ be any node in $G$ and suppose $j \tuh v \in G$. By def_3_2, $(j,v) \in E$. The directed-edges restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from def_3_1 then forces $v \in V$. This is the restrictive direction of the LN sentence “edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed”; per the addition to the LN, this is permissive and no positive existence of such an edge is asserted.

Sentence 3. Let $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By def_3_3 this means $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$, and by def_3_2 one of three cases holds.

  • $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$: then $(j_1,j_2) \in E$ by def_3_2; the restriction $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ from def_3_1 gives $j_2 \in V$, contradicting $j_2 \in J$ via the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ of def_3_1.
  • $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$: then $(j_2,j_1) \in E$ by def_3_2; the same restriction from def_3_1 gives $j_1 \in V$, contradicting $j_1 \in J$ via disjointness.
  • $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$: then $(j_1,j_2) \in L$ by def_3_2; the restriction $L \ins V \times V$ from def_3_1 gives $j_1 \in V$, again contradicting $j_1 \in J$.

Each case yields a contradiction, so no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

lean_blocks.code:
theorem no_hus_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : Node) :
    ¬ G.hus j v

theorem tuh_from_J_target_in_V (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (_hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (h : G.tuh j v) : v ∈ G.V

addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).
lean_explanation:
## Reading the signatures

### Underscored hypothesis names

The leading underscore in `_hj` and `_hne` is a Lean 4 convention telling the linter that the binder is intentionally unused inside the proof body — it is kept in the signature only to match the lecture-notes scoping (`j ∈ J`, "no *two* nodes" implying distinctness), not because the proof consumes it.

### Binder modes `( )` vs `{ }`

`no_hus_into_J` takes `(v : Node)` explicitly because the LN writes `$j \hus v \notin G$` with `v` universally quantified at the top, so callers supply `v` directly; `tuh_from_J_target_in_V` takes `{v : Node}` implicitly because the hypothesis `h : G.tuh j v` pins down `v` and Lean can infer it.

### `G.hus`, `G.tuh`, `G.adjacent` (dot notation)

These are not built-ins — `G.hus j v` is sugar for `CDMG.hus G j v`, where `hus` (head-undirected-something), `tuh` (tail-undirected-head, i.e. a directed edge `j → v`), and `adjacent` are predicates defined on the bespoke `CDMG` structure in sibling files; `hus` decomposes as the disjunction `hut ∨ huh` (arrowhead at `j` from either a directed or a bidirected edge).

### Negation as a goal

`¬ G.hus j v` in Lean 4 unfolds to `G.hus j v → False`, which is why the proof (not shown) opens with `intro h` and then derives a contradiction — the standard idiom for negated goals.

### Why no Mathlib types

Mathlib's `SimpleGraph` and `Quiver` carry no `J`/`V` partition with a bidirected layer, so `CDMG` is a bespoke structure and these theorems live entirely in CDMG-land rather than reusing a general "no incoming edges into subset" lemma.
design_choices:
**Split the LN remark into three theorems, one per sentence.** The lecture-notes remark bundles three distinct structural facts; rather than encoding a single conjunctive statement, the file exposes `no_hus_into_J`, `tuh_from_J_target_in_V`, and `no_J_J_adjacent` as separate lemmas in the order of the LN sentences. Each downstream consumer reaches for exactly one of the three, so splitting avoids forcing callers to destructure an `And`.

**Lift implicit universal quantification over `v` to top-level binders.** The operator addition `[implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause]` flags that the LN expressions $j \hus v \notin G$ and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" each quantify over $v$ implicitly. The Lean encoding makes this explicit: part 1 binds `(v : Node)` explicitly so the spelling tracks the LN word-for-word, and part 2 takes `{v : Node}` implicitly because the use-site already supplies a hypothesis `h : G.tuh j v` that determines `v`.

**Encode only the restrictive half of sentence 2.** The LN phrase "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" is permissive — presence of such edges is not asserted. So no `∃ v, G.tuh j v` clause is added; only the genuinely restrictive content `G.tuh j v → v ∈ G.V` is captured. A consumer needing a non-vacuity witness adds it locally.

**Omit `v`-membership hypotheses where edge fields already determine them.** In part 1, `v : Node` is unrestricted because `G.hus j v` already forces `v` into the graph via `hE_subset` / `hL_subset`; adding a redundant `v ∈ G` premise would only burden callers.

**Keep `hj : j ∈ G.J` in part 2 even though the proof does not consume it.** The LN sentence is scoped to $j \in J$, and downstream "source-only J" arguments already carry that fact; preserving the binder lets callers chain through without re-derivation.

**Record `hne : j1 ≠ j2` in part 3 to match the LN's "two nodes" scope.** Mathematical English's "two" implies distinctness, so the claim's natural domain is $\{(j_1, j_2) \in J \times J : j_1 \neq j_2\}$. The self-edge case `¬ G.adjacent j j` is provable but is a corollary of sentence 1, not sentence 3 — bundling them would conflate distinct LN statements, and no companion lemma is pre-added per the "no hypothetical future requirements" rule.

**Use `G.adjacent` rather than inlining `G.sus`.** Phrasing part 3 against the higher-level `adjacent` predicate (from `def_3_3`) keeps the LN vocabulary visible at the Lean level instead of exposing the three-disjunct `sus` expansion.

**Forgo mathlib graph types entirely.** `SimpleGraph`, `Quiver`, and `Digraph` carry no `J`/`V` partition with an additional bidirected layer, so each proof is a short case split on `hus` / `sus` closed by the bespoke field projections `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, and `Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj`. Future readers are explicitly warned off hunting for a phantom mathlib lemma.
